sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 36, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 35 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting pattern! SPACE converts 1 black pixel to white, but only every other press:
- SPACE 1: changed=True (B: 52→51, W: 13→14)
- SPACE 2: changed=False
- SPACE 3: changed=True (B: 51→50, W: 14→15)
- SPACE 4: changed=False
- SPACE 5: changed=True (B: 50→49, W: 15→16)
- SPACE 6: changed=False
So SPACE works every other time. The squares are at:
- Left: rows 44-47, cols 24-27
- Right: rows 49-52, cols 34-38
The black pixel count started at 52 and is now 49. I need to figure out how many total black pixels need to be converted and where the squares need to be positioned.
Let me think about this differently. The squares are close together now (cols 24-27 and 34-38). SPACE is converting black pixels to white. Maybe the goal is to convert ALL black pixels to white, or convert specific black pixels.
Since SPACE works every other press, I need to press it twice to convert one pixel. That's inefficient. Let me see if I can find positions where SPACE works every time, or if I need to move the squares between SPACE presses.
Actually, wait - maybe the pattern is that SPACE converts a pixel, then the next SPACE doesn't work because the condition is no longer met. Perhaps I need to move the squares between SPACE presses to convert more pixels.
Let me try moving the squares and then pressing SPACE again to see if different positions convert different pixels.
But first, let me understand: the black pixels are part of the container structure. Converting them to white would be "painting" or "erasing" parts of the container. Maybe the goal is to paint a path or open a passage.
Let me try a different approach: move the squares to a different position and see if SPACE converts a different pixel. This would help me understand the relationship between square position and which pixel gets converted.
Actually, I should be more efficient. Let me try to convert all remaining black pixels by repeatedly pressing SPACE and moving the squares as needed. Since SPACE works every other press, I can just keep pressing it and moving.
But wait - I'm already over the action budget. Let me think about what the most efficient approach is.
Current B count: 49 (started at 52, converted 3 so far)
If the goal is to convert all black pixels, I need 49 more conversions. At 2 SPACE presses per conversion, that's 98 more actions, which is way too many.
Maybe the goal is something else. Let me reconsider. Perhaps SPACE converts pixels that are adjacent to the squares, or in some specific relationship to them. Maybe I need to position the squares correctly first, then press SPACE once to convert all target pixels at once.
Let me try moving the squares to different positions and see if SPACE converts more pixels at once.
Actually, let me look at this from a different angle. The black pixels form the container structure. Maybe the goal is to convert specific black pixels to create a path or pattern. Let me check which pixels were converted by looking at the board before and after SPACE presses.
Let me try a more systematic approach: move the squares to various positions and press SPACE to see what gets converted. I'll track which pixels change.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# SPACE converts 1 B pixel to W every other press. Started with 52 B, now 49.
# Need to understand the pattern better. Let me check which pixels changed.

# Get current ascii to see where white pixels appeared
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Look at the area around the squares (rows 44-53, cols 20-45)
print("Area around squares:")
for i in range(40, 56):
    print(f"  {i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][20:45]}")

# Try moving squares to different position and press SPACE
# Move UP to change vertical position
action(['UP', 'UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter 2 UP:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  Sky blue: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Press SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
print("SPACE after move:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
seg = current_frame.segmentation
colors = {n['color']: n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes']}
print("Colors:", colors)

# Press SPACE again
action(['SPACE'])
print("SPACE again:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
seg = current_frame.segmentation
colors = {n['color']: n['pixels'] for n in seg['nodes']}
print("Colors:", colors)
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Converts 1 black pixel to white every other press", "LEFT": "Moves squares apart horizontally by 5 cols", "RIGHT": "Moves squares together horizontally by 5 cols", "UP": "Moves squares up by 5 rows", "DOWN": "Moves squares down by 5 rows"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black_containers": "Container structures being eroded by SPACE"}, "hud_map": {}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Two sky blue squares in containers. SPACE converts black pixels to white (1 every 2 presses). Started with 52 B pixels, now 49.", "goal_model": "Likely need to convert all or specific black pixels to white.", "recent_findings": ["SPACE works every other press", "B count: 52->51->50->49"], "open_questions": ["How many B pixels need converting?", "Does square position affect which pixel converts?"], "current_plan": "Test if moving squares changes which pixels SPACE converts.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
